media.alt: C Market Coffee Wear Red campaign — matcha spilled across red satin, the stain almost invisible
C Market Coffee Wear Red campaign — coffee blooming across white trousers in full view
C Market Coffee Wear Red campaign — a tumble down concrete steps with a dropped C Market cup
C Market Coffee Wear Red campaign — red Mary-Jane heels beside a spilled iced latte
C Market Coffee Wear Red campaign — red velvet trousers standing over a large coffee spill
C Market Coffee Wear Red campaign — a spilled C Market Coffee cup on concrete beside red fabric that hides the stain

description.label: Brief
description.text: C Market Coffee wanted a Canada Day campaign that escaped the predictable maple-leaf-and-flag promotions every café defaults to on July 1 — something witty and shareable enough to live natively on social while still driving real foot traffic and drink sales.

description.label: Insight
description.text: Two unrelated facts about the day point to the same answer: red is the colour of Canada Day, and red is the one colour that hides a coffee spill. Anyone who has carried an iced latte through a crowd knows the small daily tragedy of a stain on a white shirt — the campaign turns that universal anxiety into the reason to take part.

description.label: Direction
description.text: The creative is built as a series of candid, phone-shot "spill moments," deliberately grainy, high-contrast and off-kilter so they read as real life rather than studio advertising. Each frame pairs a C Market drink with a wardrobe outcome — matcha vanishing into red satin, coffee mortifying across white trousers, a tumble down concrete steps, red Mary-Janes beside an abandoned latte. The lockup stays minimal and consistent — wide-set caps, an italic WEAR, a single red RED — so the set coheres while the photography carries the joke. The offer rewards anyone who shows up in red.

description.label: Outcome
description.text: A generic holiday becomes a participatory promotion: customers wear red for the discount, become walking advertisements for the brand, and share the gag. The spill idea gives C Market an ownable, genuinely funny Canada Day position and a flexible image system that extends across social, in-store, and out-of-home.
